subtitle: And the Mad Dog
summary: On May 31, all the sheep, goats, and cattle in Pasteur's public experiment receive a fatal dose of anthrax, and Pasteur spends a sleepless night terrified his vaccination will fail. On June 2, an enormous crowd including senators and dignitaries gathers to witness the result: every vaccinated sheep is perfectly healthy, while twenty-two of twenty-four unvaccinated sheep lie dead or dying. It is a spectacular triumph that silences all doubters.

segments.anchor_sentence: Then on the fateful thirty-first of May all of the forty-eight sheep, two goats, and several cattle—those that were vaccinated and those to which nothing whatever had been done—all of these received a surely fatal dose of virulent anthrax bugs.

visual.scene_description: Roux kneels in the dirt injecting sheep with anthrax while Pasteur watches anxiously, surrounded by a crowd. Later that night, Pasteur tosses and turns in bed, unable to sleep.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, gaunt man with dark pointed beard in rolled-up shirtsleeves kneeling in dirt among penned sheep and cattle, holding a glass syringe, alcohol lamps and bottles on a low table beside him, a man with neat trimmed beard and round spectacles in black frock coat standing behind gripping his own hands with an expression of dread, crowd of onlookers in 19th-century French dress pressing against wooden fence rails

segments.anchor_sentence: The crowd that came to judge Pasteur on the famous second day of June, 1881, made the previous ones look like mere assemblages at country baseball games.

visual.scene_description: On June 2, a huge crowd watches as the vaccinated sheep frisk about healthily while the unvaccinated sheep lie dead in a row, with dark blood visible. Pasteur stands vindicated amid cheering spectators.
